data.title: Weitz Core Plus Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The Core Plus Income Fund’s Institutional Class returned +2.75% for the first quarter compared to a +2.78% return for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (Agg). Longer-term results (3-, 5-, and 10-year) continue to outpace the index. The primary drivers of the Fund’s outperformance for the quarter were driven by sector and security selection, led by collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), asset-backed securities (ABS) and corporate bonds.

Overview

While any quarterly commentary will be stale by the time it is published, the first quarter of 2025 might be a particular case in point after the market turbulence brought about by the “Liberation Day” tariff news on April 2, 2025. For a post-quarter commentary of the market reaction to the news of early April, please read the first quarter Fixed Income Insights letter titled “Tariff Tantrum.” Now, on to the first quarter.

Interest rates, particularly shorter-term, declined during the first quarter of 2025 despite no interest rate moves by the Federal Reserve. 2-, 3-, and 5-year Treasury rates (the part of the yield curve that most impacts the Core Plus Income Fund) peaked in early January. They then proceeded to decline from that peak to quarter-end by one-quarter of one percent (5-year Treasuries) to as much as four-tenths of one percent (3-year Treasuries). See below for our standard view of the U.S. Treasury yield curve at three different moments in time (quarter-end, year-end, and one year before quarter-end).

As measured by credit spreads, risk assets widened (increased) during the quarter. Broad investment-grade spreads (as measured by the ICE BofA) increased by 15 basis points (bps) from 82 to 97 bps. High yield spreads (as measured by the ICE BofA) widened (increased) 61 basis points from 292 to 355 bps. After many years of declines, the quarter’s higher credit spreads were (and are) a welcome change. Higher credit spreads imply a potential improvement in the risk/reward environment for deploying capital in the Core Plus Income Fund.

Portfolio Positioning

The table on the next page shows the change in allocation to various sectors, from the prior quarter and from the prior year. This summary provides a view over time of how we have allocated capital. Since our goal is to invest in sectors that we believe offer the best risk-adjusted returns, our allocations may change significantly over time.

The most notable change in sector allocation during the first quarter was a 200 bps increase in commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS). CMBS activity was led by new issue single-asset, single-borrower (SASB) investments in the office and data center sectors. Our largest investment was in SKY1 2025-LINE, a SASB transaction collateralized by a single, floating rate, partially amortizing loan, secured by a first lien mortgage on a portfolio of six high quality, Class A, LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) Gold certified office properties located across four submarkets of the states of California and Washington. The properties are strategically located in technology-centric markets and have received a cumulative $125 million of capital expenditures since 2017 to remain competitive with updated amenity packages. Property tenants include investment-grade companies Amazon, Riot Games, GitHub, and Nestlé. In addition, we believe the transaction was structured with conservative leverage and strong debt service coverage. We expect to earn above-average returns over our 2-5 year investment horizon.

We also added two new rated feeder transactions from Brightwood and Guggenheim, consisting primarily of middle-market loans to private companies. In both transactions, we invested at the senior most portion of the capital structure. In addition, we added agency MBS, longer-duration Treasury securities, corporate bonds, and a wide variety of investments within ABS and commercial real estate (CRE) CLOs.

Portfolio Metrics

The Fund’s average effective maturity declined to 9.4 years as of March 31, 2025, from 9.5 years as of December 31, 2024, while our average effective duration decreased to 5.5 years from 5.8 years over the same period, compared to the Agg’s average effective duration of 6.0 years on March 31. These measures provide a guide to the Fund’s interest rate sensitivity. A higher average effective duration increases the Fund’s price sensitivity to changes in interest rates (either up or down).

As of March 31, our high-yield exposure as a percentage of net assets was 4.7%, up from 3.9% on December 31, 2024. The Fund can invest up to 25% of net assets in high yield, therefore we have ample capacity to take advantage of valuation discrepancies/ opportunities in the high yield area.

The Fund’s yield to worst (YTW) remains higher than the Agg, while the Fund’s interest rate risk (duration) continues to be lower. Constructing a portfolio that produces “carry” – income achieved from regular coupon payments – (via the YTW measurement) and lower interest rate sensitivity (via lower duration) is a feature of our flexible mandate and ability to invest in other higher quality securities that reside outside of the benchmark. The Fund’s 30-day SEC yield was 5.07% (subsidized) and 5.00% (unsubsidized) as of March 31, 2025 (subsidized yield reflects fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements, while unsubsidized yield does not).

Top Quarterly Contributors

  • U.S. Treasury Bonds: U.S. Treasuries were the largest contributor to performance. With a duration of over 10 years, our Treasury portfolio experienced unrealized mark-to-market gains as interest rates declined across the curve during the quarter.
  • Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS): Agency MBS was the second largest contributor to performance as lower interest rates and carry more than offset wider mortgage-backed spreads during the quarter.
  • Asset Backed Securities: Our ABS portfolio was a positive contributor to performance due to high coupon income and price appreciation, as ABS spreads tightened throughout the year.

Top Quarterly Detractors

There were no material detractors to fund performance during the first quarter.

Fund Strategy Revisited

Our investment approach consists primarily of investing in a diversified portfolio of high-quality bonds while maintaining an overall portfolio average duration of 3.5 to 7 years. When attractively priced, we may seek to capture attractive coupon income and potential price appreciation by investing in longer-duration and lower-quality bonds. We may also invest up to 25% in fixed income securities that are not considered investment grade (such as high yield and convertible bonds as well as preferred and convertible preferred stock), and we do so when we perceive the risk/reward characteristics to be favorable.

We do not, and will not, try to mimic any index as we construct our portfolio. We believe our flexible mandate and high-conviction portfolio will benefit investors long term. We utilize a bottom-up, research-driven approach and select portfolio assets one security at a time based on our view of opportunities in the marketplace. Our fixed income research is not dependent on, but often benefits from, the due diligence work our equity teammates conduct on companies and industries.

Overall, we strive to be adequately compensated for the risks assumed to maximize investment (or reinvestment) yield and to avoid making interest rate bets, particularly those that depend on interest rates going down. We have often maintained a lower duration profile than the index, particularly in very low-yield environments. Our shorter duration profile has benefited shareholders in periods of rising interest rates.

Maintaining a liquid (e.g., Treasuries and agency MBS) and overall, up-in-quality portfolio positioning that we believe can generate higher income returns than respective indexes positions us well as we begin a new year–a year that could get messy in so many unpredictable ways. But it could also be one that will particularly allow us to lean into and be liquidity providers if/when more favorable risk/reward opportunities present themselves.

We are thankful for the trust and partnership many have in our firm and its investment process.

Non-Investment Grade Bonds are those securities (commonly referred to as “high yield” or “junk” bonds) rated BB+ and below by one or more credit ratings agencies. Yield to worst (YTW) is the lowest potential yield that can be received on a bond portfolio without the underlying issuers defaulting. data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 Portfolio 2029-2032 Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary1 Highlights

  • In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) recorded a mid-single-digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid February.
  • Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, especially related to trade and tariffs.
  • The Fed kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024.

Market review and outlook

In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index recorded a mid-single digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February. Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs, and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs. Amid rising volatility, the index gained 2.78% in January before falling in February (−1.30%) and March (-5.63%). It was particularly dragged down by growth stocks and small-cap stocks.

During the quarter, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024. The central bank indicated that further reductions would depend on real progress on inflation or unexpected weakness in labor. New data showed that inflation climbed 2.80% in February from a year earlier-higher than the Fed's 2.00% target-as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers continued to slip, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead.

Against this backdrop, the S&P 500 Index returned -4.27% in the first quarter. However, international developed-market stocks gained 6.94%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, buoyed partly by strength in Germany ( +16.00% ), where February brought elections and optimism that the new administration led by Friedrich Merz would pursue a pro-growth agenda. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 2.93% advance. Some emerging European markets, including Poland (+31%), Greece (+23%), and the Czech Republic (+29%), posted strong returns, bolstered by an improved outlook for the eurozone following Germany's plans to boost spending on defense and infrastructure. China (+15%) was also a standout performer; however, Taiwan (-13%) and India (-3.00%) were relatively weak.

Investment-grade bonds rebounded during the quarter as U.S. Treasury yields eased. Long-term Treasuries and Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities did particularly well amid growing economic growth and inflation concerns. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.78%.

Almost every measure of consumer and business sentiment, or soft data, has notably deteriorated amid rising uncertainty around trade policy. While sentiment usually lags hard data, such as employment and spending, today's soft data signals a far more dire outlook than the hard data implies. The real concern is whether businesses and consumers will continue to face this level of uncertainty for a prolonged period. With trade disputes lasting for nearly a year and a half during President Trump's first term, the sentiment could be right this time around, foreshadowing a deeper economic slowdown. Given the heightened risk, we continued to lower U.S. equity exposure while adding to European equities on an upside potential related to Germany's increased fiscal spending on defense and infrastructure and a more unified commitment from leaders across the eurozone on improving competitiveness.

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio (MUTF:JENAX) recorded a modest gain for the quarter and outpaced its blended benchmark, comprising a 38.50% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index, a 16.50% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net), and a 45.00% weighting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. An out-of-benchmark stake in the Real Assets Fund (T. Rowe Price) notably contributed for the quarter, as this asset class outperformed core equities, driven by the precious metals and energy groups. Exposure to non-U.S. stocks also added value in a period during which U.S. equities lagged. International Growth Fund (John Hancock) was a key relative contributor, aided by security selection in the industrials sector. Equity Income Fund (T. Rowe Price) also provided a relative performance boost, largely on the strength of security selection within industrials and consumer staples. On the other hand, Spectrum Income Fund (T. Rowe Price) detracted, mainly due to exposure to high-yield bonds, U.S. dollar-hedged international bonds, and floating-rate loans.

data.title: VNQ: If You Think REITs Are Safe, It's Time To Rethink (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
VNQ ETF: Previous thesis and new developments

My last analysis on the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VNQ) was published back in October 2024. In an article entitled “VNQ: This Chart Shows It's Not Too Late To Buy REITs,” I argued for a buy thesis based on its high dividend yield at that time. More specifically, I argued that:

The REIT sector has recently outperformed the SP500 by a large margin. However, VNQ's most recent dividend payouts suggest its price advancements have yet to catch up with earnings growth. VNQ's latest quarterly dividend distribution made in October translates into a 50% YOY growth when returned capital is excluded and 27% growth on a TTM basis. VNQ is currently still trading at its most attractive levels in a decade and remains the top ranked sector fund on our radar.

Since then, there have been a few material changes to our macroscopic landscape. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top one that is most relevant to VNQ and also REIT stocks in general: the likelihood of persisting high interest rates. Due to a series of developments ranging from inflation data and also the ongoing trade disputes, interest rates have increased substantially since my last writing.

Take 10-year treasury rates (US10Y) as an example. The reading has increased from about 3.75% at the time of my last analysis to the current level of around 4.4%. In early May, when Moody’s Ratings announced the downgrade of the U.S. credit rating, 10-year rates pushed above 4.5%. Rates with longer durations increased even more with 30-year rates temporarily peaking above 5%.

Under these conditions, the market now expects large odds for interest rates to stay elevated, making REIT funds like VNQ untimely due to their holding’s reliance on debt financing. These considerations have also caused me to downgrade my rating on VNQ to hold, as detailed next.

VNQ ETF: Interest rate outlook

Looking ahead, the market’s current expectation points to far higher odds for interest rates to stay elevated in the near future. To be more explicit, the next chart is provided by CME Group FedWatch Tool, which shows the current probability of interest rate cuts for the rest of 2025. As seen, currently, the most likely scenario that the market expects is that they will lower rates to the 3.75% to 4.00% range by the end of the year. And the expected probability is 39.8%. Compared to the expectations only a month ago, this new outlook is far less aggressive. A month ago, the market expected deeper cuts to the range of 3.25% to 3.5% range, with a higher probability of 41.6%.

VNQ ETF: Basic facts

With the overall background provided thus far, the rest of the discussion will be more specific to VNQ. Thus, let me first cover some basics of the fund to facilitate the rest of the analysis. VNQ is one of the largest and also most popular ETFs with targeted exposure to publicly traded REIT stocks as detailed in its fund description below:

VNQ fund description: The fund’s objective is to provide a high level of income and moderate long-term capital appreciation by tracking the performance of a benchmark index that measures the performance of publicly traded equity REITs and other real estate-related investments. The benchmark index is the MSCI US Investable Market Real Estate 25/50 Index. The fund is passively managed and uses a full-replication approach. The fund features low expenses to minimize net tracking error.

The fund’s indexing method is clearly reflected in its top holdings as seen below. Its largest holdings represent broad exposure to various sub-sectors ranging from telecommunication towers, storage, malls, etc. To wit, its largest 4 individual holdings are American Tower Corp. (accounting for 6.25% of holdings), Prologis, Inc. (5.61%), Welltower Inc. (5.52%), and Equinix, Inc. (4.79%).

VNQ ETF: Downturn performance

Given VNQ’s focus on REIT stocks as discussed above, many investors consider VNQ as a “safer” investment. There are certainly good reasons here (and I will elaborate more on this a bit later). But I urge investors to reexamine this assumption under current conditions. I believe the developments since my last writing have made a market downturn more likely.

Historically, VNQ has suffered deeper and longer drawdowns than the S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY), as you can see from the following two charts below. There are a couple of reasons that can help us understand this. The top one on my list is again leveraged, especially during times with elevated borrowing rates as aforementioned. Real estate properties are an asset class that often involves higher leverage than the overall economy. Leverage, of course, amplifies returns in good times, but also magnifies losses during downturns. A second reason that can make VNQ suffer larger drawdowns than SP500 is the perception of liquidity. The major assets of REIT stocks are physical properties, which are often less liquid, especially during market downturns, compared to other types of assets.

Other risks and final thoughts

Besides the issue of borrowing rates, a couple of other risks are worth mentioning. As aforementioned, VNQ is one of the largest and most popular REIT funds, as I know. As you can see from the following chart, VNQ has a net expense ratio of 0.13% and approximately $63.1 billion in total net assets, far exceeding its peers. However, a few competing funds are becoming worthy alternatives in terms of expense ratios and assets under management (AUM). Notably, the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) features an expense ratio of 0.09%, significantly lower than VNQ. With a substantial AUM of $7.28 billion, XLRE also offers sufficient liquidity to meet most investors’ needs. The Fidelity MSCI Real Estate Index ETF (FREL) boasts a very competitive expense ratio of 0.08% and also manages a sizable AUM of $1.04 billion in assets.

To echo a comment made earlier, there are good reasons that many investors consider VNQ as a “safer” investment. The leading factor is the generous dividend payouts. As you can see from the following chart, currently, the dividend yield of VNQ stands at 4.07%, a quite attractive level by either horizontal (say compared to treasury rates) or vertical comparison (i.e., compared to its own historical levels). For example, in the past 10 years, the average historical yield of VNQ is 3.99%. The current yield is thus slightly above the historical average, indicating an attractive valuation. Also note that despite some extreme data points, VNQ's dividend yield has largely fluctuated with a relatively narrow range in the long term between about 2.5% and 4.5%, an indicator of the fund’s stability.

To recap, this revisit to VNQ is triggered by the change in our interest rate outlook since my last writing. Due to a series of geopolitical and economic events since then, interest rates have increased substantially since my last writing and are likely to remain elevated. I expect these changes to pressure REIT funds like VNQ and suggest investors reexamine their assumption of their downside protection should a market downturn materialize. These considerations have led me to lower my rating on VNQ to hold under current conditions.

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 Portfolio 2025-28 Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary1

Highlights

  • In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) recorded a mid-single-digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February.
  • Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, especially related to trade and tariffs.
  • The Fed kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024.

Market review and outlook

In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index recorded a mid-single digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February. Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs, and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs. Amid rising volatility, the index gained 2.78% in January before falling in February (−1.30%) and March (-5.63%). It was particularly dragged down by growth stocks and small-cap stocks.

During the quarter, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024. The central bank indicated that further reductions would depend on real progress on inflation or unexpected weakness in labor. New data showed that inflation climbed 2.80% in February from a year earlier-higher than the Fed's 2.00% target-as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers continued to slip, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead.

Against this backdrop, the S&P 500 Index returned -4.27% in the first quarter. However, international developed-market stocks gained 6.94%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, buoyed partly by strength in Germany (+16.00%), where February brought elections and optimism that the new administration led by Friedrich Merz would pursue a pro-growth agenda. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 2.93% advance. Some emerging European markets, including Poland (+31%), Greece (+23%), and the Czech Republic (+29%), posted strong returns, bolstered by an improved outlook for the eurozone following Germany's plans to boost spending on defense and infrastructure. China (+15%) was also a standout performer; however, Taiwan (-13%) and India (-3.00%) were relatively weak.

Investment-grade bonds rebounded during the quarter as U.S. Treasury yields eased. Long-term Treasuries and Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities did particularly well amid growing economic growth and inflation concerns. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.78%.

Almost every measure of consumer and business sentiment, or soft data, has notably deteriorated amid rising uncertainty around trade policy. While sentiment usually lags hard data, such as employment and spending, today's soft data signals a far more dire outlook than the hard data implies. The real concern is whether businesses and consumers will continue to face this level of uncertainty for a prolonged period. With trade disputes lasting for nearly a year and a half during President Trump's first term, the sentiment could be right this time around, foreshadowing a deeper economic slowdown. Given the heightened risk, we continued to lower U.S. equity exposure while adding to European equities on an upside potential related to Germany's increased fiscal spending on defense and infrastructure and a more unified commitment from leaders across the eurozone on improving competitiveness.

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a modest gain for the quarter and outpaced its blended benchmark, comprising a 22.93% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index, a 9.82% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net), a 48.00% weighting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, and a 19.25% weighting in the Bloomberg 1-5 Year TIPS Index. An out-of-benchmark stake in the Real Assets Fund (T. Rowe Price) contributed for the quarter, as this asset class outperformed core equities, driven by the precious metals and energy groups. Exposure to non-U.S. stocks also added value in a period during which U.S. equities lagged. International Growth Fund (John Hancock) was a key relative contributor, aided by security selection in the industrials sector. Equity Income Fund (T. Rowe Price) also provided a relative performance boost, largely on the strength of security selection within industrials and consumer staples. On the other hand, Spectrum Income Fund (T. Rowe Price) detracted, mainly due to exposure to high-yield bonds, U.S. dollar-hedged international bonds, and floating-rate loans.

data.title: FEPI Vs. QDTE: Both Funds Offer A Unique Value Proposition
data.description:
Overview

With so many new income-focused funds available to investors now, it can be difficult to know which ones offer the most compelling value proposition. Option ETFs receive a bit of a mixed reputation as they're the cause of debate on whether or not these funds actually create value for investors. Speaking from personal experience, I've been able to implement my own strategy that now pays me dividends on a weekly basis, with REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:FEPI) and Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (BATS:QDTE) as part of that strategy.

Each of these funds implements different strategies but the underlying objective is the same: to harness the volatility of the markets using options and generate additional income for investors. I've maintained a position in FEPI since April of 2024 and now that it's been a year since I started my position, I wanted to measure its performance. I hold a handful of different option ETFs with varying levels of risk, strategies, and distribution rates and I always considered FEPI an 'anchor fund' because it actually held shares of the underlying equities it writes options against.

On the other hand, I only recently initiated a position in QDTE and when backtesting the performance, it seems to slightly outpace FEPI so far. Although QDTE's approach may have a bit of an elevated risk profile in comparison to FEPI, certain performance metrics make me question whether or not FEPI actually serves a purpose in the income portion of my portfolio. Therefore, I wanted to create this comparison piece and share some of the data points that I have come across. Ultimately, I still believe that both FEPI and QDTE are great option ETFs to own. There's absolutely no reason that investors can't own both, but this may be helpful for investors who want to consolidate some of their holdings and maximize the efficiency of capital.

FEPI Fund Strategy

According to FEPI's latest overview, the fund has total assets of about $455M that are spread across 15 different holdings. FEPI offers an estimated annual distribution rate of slightly over 25% and distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. The fund has an inception dating back to October of 2023 and has a management fee of 0.65%. What makes FEPI unique is that it actually holds common shares of the equities that it writes options against. In theory, this should reduce the overall price volatility and help the fund retain its NAV a lot better.

When looking at the top positions, we can see that Tesla (TSLA) is the largest holding at 7.65%. This is followed by exposure to many other large-cap technology companies, such as Apple (AAPL), Netflix (NFLX), Palantir (PLTR), Meta Platforms (META), and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) just to name a few. While FEPI doesn't specifically encompass all of the holdings that are within the Nasdaq, the daily price movements can be similar at times due to a lot of similar holdings.

FEPI implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) option strategy that harnesses the volatility of these stocks, while also allowing FEPI to participate in a greater amount of capital appreciation. Since the strategy requires volatility in order to be efficient, it makes sense why FEPI focuses on these large-cap players in the Technology sector. I do like the OTM strategy because it increases the possibility of FEPI's price rising during the growth of the Technology sector over the next decade.

To demonstrate how the option strategy works, let's imagine that FEPI owns an asset that is priced at $50 per share. In order to generate income, FEPI will write an option against this asset and may select a strike price that is typically between 5% and 15% above the current market price. So in this example, let's imagine that FEPI selects a strike price of $55 per share. If the underlying asset has a strong earnings report and the stock price increases to $60 per share, FEPI's option will be executed at the strike price and the fund will receive a premium in addition to the upside experienced. However, FEPI will only participate in the price growth up to the strike price of $55 per share and everything above this is essentially sacrificed in exchange for the option premium collected.

This is exactly why it's common to see FEPI underperform its own holdings over a longer period of time. Despite implementing an OTM strategy, there should be limited expectations for growth. Just to demonstrate, I pulled in a few of the top holdings alongside FEPI. FEPI has a negative price return of 16.58%, despite having exposure to Palantir, which appreciated by more than 590%! This is a natural weakness of these option ETFs and I would only suggest utilizing FEPI as an accompanying position within an already diversified portfolio of assets.

QDTE Fund Strategy

According to QDTE's overview, the fund has total assets of about $754M. The fund has an inception dating back to March of 2024 and has a gross expense ratio of 0.97%. So in comparison, FEPI has a less expensive management fee and a slightly longer period of operating history. The key difference here is that unlike FEPI, QDTE doesn't actually own any of the underlying common shares of the index it aims to track. QDTE utilizes 0DTE (zero-day-to-expiry) options on the Innovation-100 Index. This is confirmed when looking at the actual list of holdings below. We can see that there are no equities listed, but there are several call options with their respective expiry dates.

Therefore, QDTE implements a synthetic option strategy and this has the potential to increase the price volatility that the fund experiences over time. The main benefit of this synthetic approach is that the option premiums are a bit higher, which leads to a higher annual distribution rate of 35.4%. A 10% difference in distribution yield can be a major driver in which fund an investor will ultimately choose.

Another key difference here is that the distributions from QDTE are paid out on a weekly basis, which makes this more attractive for investors who want to collect income more frequently. So technically, someone with a lot of free capital would be able to receive paycheck-like distributions every single week.

Since QDTE aims to replicate the daily movement of the Innovation-100 Index, the price movement of QDTE should be more closely tied to the Nasdaq. However, the combination of a more generous distribution yield and the synthetic strategy, means that the NAV erosion is more prevalent here with QDTE. Looking at a quick comparison of QDTE against a more traditional index-tracking ETF like Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), we can see how QDTE severely underperforms from a price perspective.

I have an existing position in QQQ and I view QDTE as a way to extract some income from my existing position in QQQ without needing to sell. I plan to hold QQQ for at least another decade and the path forward isn't likely to be straight up. There will be moments of decline, higher volatility, and uncertainty, which is why I believe QDTE to be a great way to capitalize on that.

Performance Comparison

When doing a straightforward total return comparison, we can see that both funds underperform QQQ as expected. However, I was a bit surprised to see that QDTE had more than twice the total return of FEPI since its inception. QDTE has a total return of 13.8%, which outpaces FEPI's total return of only 5.8%. Most of all, it seems like QDTE was also able to participate in a greater amount of capital appreciation during those periods of positive market momentum.

In order to dig a bit deeper, I also wanted to compare how the two funds performed during market declines. The only real period of decline we can reference would be the initial market's reaction to President Trump's tariff proposals. We can see that both FEPI and QDTE declined alongside the rise of the market, nearly falling at an equal pace. The difference here was that FEPI's price was able to recover better on a YTD basis.

However, this shifts when we compare QDTE's recovery from its absolute bottom. It seems like QDTE was able to see a greater price increase from the bottom of April 21st. FEPI relies on its 15 holdings to fuel the price growth of the fund, whereas QDTE's growth is essentially linked to the Nasdaq. Although, I'll admit that these very time-specific comparisons may be a bit trivial in the greater scheme of it all. The underlying option strategies of both funds cap the upside price growth and can lead to NAV erosion over time.

For instance, if we just compare the price change of both FEPI and QDTE, we can see that both funds have still suffered from declines from inception. As expected, FEPI has remained a little bit more resilient since the distribution policy isn't as generous as QDTE's. Since FEPI actually holds common shares of the equities it writes options against, this also slows the rate of the NAV deterioration that can be experienced. The recent market decline driven by trade wars actually provided a bit of an insight into some of the worst conditions for these types of option ETFs. You'll notice below that the price of both of these funds has remained on par with one another before March 2025.

Distribution Comparison

The obvious difference between both funds is that FEPI offers an estimated 25% distribution rate, while QDTE's distribution is higher at ~35%. Quick napkin math tells us that:

$10,000 Invested

  1. $2,500 in annual distributions received from FEPI.
  2. $3,500 in annual distributions received from QDTE.

Although it may be seen as a bit of a gimmick, I tend to like the weekly payouts that QDTE provides. I personally believe that the weekly distributions increase the flexibility and utility of the income. I typically implement a dividend wheel strategy, which basically means that I take the distributions from QDTE and manually reinvest them into another area of my portfolio. For instance, I've been allocating a lot of these weekly distributions to growth companies like ASML Holdings (ASML), Microsoft (MSFT), and Nvidia (NVDA) to help me accumulate a bit of shares.

However, FEPI does have more consistent payouts every month. Looking back at the dividend history, payouts have remained within a tight range, with the only decreases happening in recent months as the market declined.

In comparison, we can see QDTE's payouts are a lot less consistent as there are big swings in the payout amounts. If you are an investor who prefers a more predictable and consistent stream of income, FEPI may be the better option.

FEPI's most recent section 19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the distributions paid YTD have been funded by the return of capital. Similarly, QDTE's section 19(a) notice also indicates that 100% of the distributions paid YTD were funded by the return of capital. The benefit here is that return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore aren't taxed as such. Instead, return of capital distributions reduce an investor's cost basis and this allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

For example, let's imagine that you buy a stock that trades at $10 per share. Therefore, your cost basis would be $10 per share at the time of purchase. Let's now imagine that the same stock pays out a $1 dividend using the return of capital. As a result, your initial cost basis would now be reduced to $9. This process can technically continue until your cost basis reaches $0. At that point, all distributions would be considered to be long-term capital gains. So we could say that both FEPI and QDTE are tax-efficient funds that can be utilized within a regular brokerage account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, both FEPI and QDTE offer a compelling value proposition of high-income potential and exposure to the movement of Tech. While each of these funds offers its own unique benefits, tradeoffs, risk profiles, and income potential, I am leaning more in favor of QDTE. QDTE has outperformed from a total return perspective while offering a similar price movement to FEPI over the last year. While there are some small price differences that were outlined here, these differences were marginal in my opinion. While I don't plan to sell out of my existing FEPI position, I also don't plan to increase my position. The fact that FEPI actually holds underlying shares of the equities it writes options against could lead to outperformance against QDTE during a stronger bull market. However, QDTE will likely outperform during sideways trading markets as the distributions are much higher.

data.title: AAR Corp.: Margin Expansion And Aerospace & Defense MRO Growth Offer Attractive Upside
data.description:
AAR Corporation (AIR), a leading provider of MRO (maintenance, repair and overhaul) services for the commercial aviation and defense sectors, saw its share price decline sharply after posting Q3 2025 earnings that missed Wall Street estimates. As part of my broader coverage of the aerospace and defense industry, I have maintained a Buy rating on AAR, even as the company’s recent performance has lagged expectations. Since my last report, AIR stock has declined 3.7%, underperforming the S&P 500’s 3.2% gain.

In this report, I review AAR’s Q3 2025 earnings, update my price target, and explain why long-term fundamentals continue to support a buy rating despite short-term weakness.

AAR Sales Double Digit Growth Is Not Enough For Wall Street

That the stock fell post earnings may actually be somewhat surprising. Wall Street expected third quarter sales of $699.1 million and AAR reported sales of “only” $678.2 million. So, the company definitely did miss on revenues, but reported revenues still marked a 20% increase in sales. EBITDA grew 39% to $81.2 million indicating margin expansion from 10.3% to 12%. This margin expansion was realized despite some lower efficiencies related to higher headcounts on its facilities in Dallas and Wellington, Kansas. These sites will take over the work performed at the Long Island facility which the company will exit in Q1 2026.

Parts Supply sales grew 12% to $270 million generating $36.8 million on stable EBITDA margins. Repair & Engineering sales grew 53% to $215.9 million driven by the acquisition of Product Support from Triumph Group (TGI). EBITDA grew 110% to $27.9 million on margins expanding from 9.4% to 12.9%.

Integrated Solutions sales declined by 1.5% on lower government sales, but driven by the strong performance of Trax EBITDA was up 11% to $16.2 million. So, we saw a 1.1 percent point increase in margins almost fully attributable to Trax, which is the company’s maintenance management software.

So, the earnings were not bad, but the reality is that Wall Street expected stronger growth. The shortfall is likely caused by Used Serviceable Materials sales recorded in the Parts Supply segment. In prior quarters, securing enough feedstock for this business has become challenging. The reason is that commercial airplanes and engines are high in demand by airline operators and that means that instead of selling assets to an MRO provider, these assets remain in service with airlines and limit the growth prospects of USM. At the same time, Q3 was somewhat different in the sense that USM growth did not fully materialize due to lower demand for USM from engine maintenance shops. So, on one hand, we have the more structural challenge of low feedstock availability and on the other hand, we had a transient pressure in Q3 from the engine maintenance shop demand. On the plus side, the company did report an increase of $22 million in whole asset sales which capitalizes on demand for commercial airplanes and engines.

While the market definitely has not been impressed with the results, I do believe they were quite decent. The company also brought down its leverage from 3.6x in March 2024 to 3.1x by Q3 2025 and is targeting a leverage of 2x to 2.5x. Growing EBITDA is definitely going to help with that.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For AAR?

AAR has several risks and opportunities. The major opportunity, of course, is that demand for MRO and parts remains elevated. The drawback is that feedstock for the USM sales is limited to the shortage of new airplanes. Instead of airplanes being sold to MRO providers to drive their USM sales, they are now continuing in commercial service. So, the airplane shortage is a two-edged sword. On one hand, it drives demand for MRO and parts and, on the other hand, it reduces part availability.

Furthermore, we do note that the company is operating against its capacity limits. This leaves a lot of upsides in the MRO business to be realized through efficiency gains. The new sites to be launched in Miami and Oklahoma should boost capacity, but the risk there is of course, any delay in getting those sites operational and ramped to fully capacity.

On its process of deleveraging, we note that the company has nearly $1 billion in debt maturing in FY2028-FY2029, which it will likely need to refinance or refinance partially.

The divesture of the non-core Landing Gear Overhaul business should help the company in bringing its gross and net debt down.

AAR Stock Is An Underperformer With Upside

For AAR Corp., I am increasing the price target from $74.15 for FY25 to $79.56. The net debt leverage (including lease debt) is expected to remain high this year at 3.3x, but improve significantly in the years ahead. As mentioned earlier, it is inevitable that a portion of its debt maturities in FY2028 and FY2029 has to be refinanced, but I do expect that leverage to come down to 1.9x by the end of FY27. With the expansion of the MRO footprint through adding new locations and the acquisition of the Product Support business, I do expect a significant increase in EBITDA and more so in free cash flow, supporting a bullish view on the stock.

While I normally map price targets a year ahead as I believe the market should be forward-looking, we see that AAR has not always delivered and therefore adopting the conservative target for the current financial year is appropriate and this provides 28% upside while the stock remains undervalued compared to peers and end market demand remains healthy with a larger company footprint to service demand in the years ahead.

Conclusion: Upside Remains As AAR Business Scales

It is undeniable that AAR is facing some pressures, particularly on its USM sales. That has become a reason for the stock price to plunge in response to the Q3 earnings. The company also faces a debt load that it more than likely will have to refinance. Ideally, we see companies being able to service debt from their cash pile, but for AAR that is not the case. So, there are some risks. However, the growth that the company has in the years ahead for the medium to long-term boasts well and provides sufficient reason to reiterate my buy rating and even increase my price target.

data.title: 5 Alpha Generating Monsters
data.description:
Finding stocks that consistently generate positive alpha—meaning they outperform the market on a risk-adjusted basis—is a key goal for savvy investors. While markets can be unpredictable, certain companies have demonstrated a persistent ability to beat benchmarks through strong fundamentals, competitive advantages, and flawless execution. In this article, we highlight five standout stocks that have not only delivered market-beating returns but have also maintained positive alpha over extended periods, making them compelling choices for long-term portfolios.

Screen 1 – Sortino ratio

The Sortino ratio measures returns relative to downside risk. It’s a valuable metric because many investors are risk-averse, and when a stock has a history of large drawdowns, the Sortino ratio tends to be lower than it is for a stock with moderate drawdowns. A Sortino ratio of 1 indicates that the stock in question has experienced drawdowns that are roughly in line with its benchmark. A Sortino ratio higher than 1 indicates that when the benchmark declines, the stock declines to a lesser degree. This is the genesis of alpha.

I calculated the Sortino ratios for 250 stocks that had outperformed their benchmark over the last five years. I then eliminated those who scored less than 1.25. 83 stocks passed this screen.

Screen 2 – Alpha consistency

The second screen looked at how consistently these 83 stocks beat their benchmarks. I only considered those that generated positive alpha for 4 out of the last 5 years – from 2020 through 2024. The idea here was that any stock, no matter how much alpha it generates, can have a bad year, but I only wanted to include those who had one bad year out of the last five years. This screen yielded 71 names.

Screen 3 – Margin of Safety

The next screen looked at valuation. In most cases, stocks that deliver consistent alpha over a 5-year period are going to gain the attention of the analyst community and investors in general. They tend to trade at premium prices. So, to avoid overpaying for these stocks, I screened for the Margin of Safety, which is the difference between the stock’s market price and its fair value. For my purposes, I used a discounted cash flow method to arrive at fair value. I set the bar for the minimum MOS at 10%, which means that they are trading at a 10% discount. 36 stocks passed this screen.

Screen 4 – Earnings growth

In my last screen, I looked for positive earnings growth estimates for the next few years. I want to invest in companies that are expected to grow their earnings by a minimum of 10% per year. 28 stocks passed this screen.

The Results

For this article, I will highlight the top 5 stocks out of the 28 that passed all 4 screens. These stocks are growing their earnings at 10% or higher, are trading at reasonable prices, have shown consistency in generating alpha, and have good risk-adjusted returns. Here are the five stocks that I call Alpha Monsters.

Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO)

  • Sector: Financial Services.
  • Industry: Asset Management.
  • Specialties: Private Equity, Credit, Real Assets, Retirement Services.
  • AUM: $650 billion.
  • 4 out of 5 positive alpha years.
  • 1.31 Sortino ratio.
  • 32% Margin of Safety.
  • 22.2% 5-year annual earnings growth estimate.
  • Seeking Alpha analysts rating: Buy.

Coca-Cola Consolidated, Inc. (COKE)

  • Sector: Consumer Defensive.
  • Industry: Beverages – Non-Alcoholic.
  • Market Cap: $10 billion.
  • 4 out of 5 positive alpha years.

Enova International, Inc. (ENVA)

Fair Isaac Corporation (FICO)

  • 22% Margin of Safety.
  • 25.1% 5-year annual earnings growth estimate.
  • Seeking Alpha analysts rating: Buy.

Final Thoughts

These 5 stocks are a rare breed. They produce consistently positive alpha; they’re growing earnings; they are not overpriced; and they hold up well when the market goes down. So, what should you do with these 5 names? I can think of a few things.

First, don’t rush out and buy them based solely on this article. As always, do your own investigation into any company that looks good at first glance. Second, if one or more of them pass your scrutiny, look into how they will impact your overall asset allocation. If you’re already overweight in Financial Services, for instance, you don’t need two more names there. But you might be able to do an upgrade by selling something you’re holding now and buying something new that has better numbers.

And third, if you listen to the 45% of economists who believe that a recession is likely within the next 12 months, the market will probably go back down and re-test the April low. If you are worried that this may happen, just put these names on your watch list and see if you can pick up a name or two at significantly better prices.

On the other hand, if you’re convinced that the worst is over and the market is headed for new highs, then do your research, vet these names, and buy the ones that pass your tests.

data.title: Nickel Miners News For The Month Of May 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the nickel miners news for May.

The past month saw nickel prices slightly lower and LME inventory slightly lower.

Nickel price news

As of May 30, the nickel spot price was $15,354/t, slightly lower from $15,544/tonne last month.

Nickel inventory at the London Metals Exchange [LME] was slightly lower the past month at 200,862 tonnes (201,426 tonnes last month).

Nickel spot price 10 year chart - Current price = $15,354/t (source)

Nickel demand vs. supply chart

S&P Global Market Intelligenceforecastst nickel oversupply to 2027, then a 'primary' nickel deficit starting in 2028 (as of June 2024) (source)

Carbon Credits forecasts a nickel oversupply to 2030, then growing deficits from 2030-2035 (as of May 2025) (source)

Trend Investing vs. IEA demand forecast for EV metals (Trend Investing) (IEA)

2022 — IEA forecasts 60 new nickel mines needed by 2030

Lithium & Nickel are the two 'high importance to energy' critical materials in the medium term (2025-2035) (source - page 29)

Nickel Market News

On May 21, Fastmarkets reported:

Nickel prices stagnate amid oversupply and limited supply response from Indonesia...Data from the International Nickel Study Group points to the nickel market remaining heavily oversubscribed in early 2025, and Fastmarkets forecasts a third consecutive annual market surplus above 100,000 tonnes in 2025. With such fundamentals, it is difficult to envision any sustained recovery in the nickel price unless demand growth accelerates, or the supply side shows greater discipline.

On May 22, Discovery Alert reported:

Nickel price forecast: Key factors driving 2025 market dynamics...Despite widespread discussion of market surpluses, exchange inventories have shown a notable decline in recent weeks. This contradiction between market narrative and actual inventory levels suggests underlying strength in consumption patterns that may not be fully recognized in current nickel price forecast models...LME and SHFE warehouses have reported a combined 8% reduction in nickel inventories over the past six weeks... Indonesian ore prices have risen to near two-year highs, approaching $48 per wet metric tonne for 1.8% nickel content... "Indonesia has both the means and motivation to support higher nickel prices. At current levels, some of the country's less efficient producers are barely breaking even, creating pressure for government intervention in mining"...

Nickel Company News

Producers

Norilsk Nickel (Nornickel)

On May 21, Nornickel announced: "More power, less emissions: Nornickel completes modernization of CHP-2 in Talnakh."

On May 22, Nornickel announced: "Nornickel’s board of directors recommends not to pay dividends for FY 2024 to shareholders."

Vale SA (NYSE:VALE)

Vale Voisey's Bay Mine is a key Canadian and global source of nickel. Vale plans a refinery at Bécancour in Québec to supply GM with battery grade nickel sulfate with deliveries targeted to commence in H2 2026.

On May 12, Market Screener reported: "CICC initiates Vale S.A. at outperform with $11.30 Price Target."

On May 16, PT Vale Indonesia announced:

PT Vale Indonesia Tbk holds 2025 Annual General Meeting of shareholders...The year 2024 was a transformational year for PT Vale. Despite global economic headwinds and declining nickel prices, the Company recorded resilient performance...The Company recorded the lowest cash cost of sales in three years at US$9,374 per ton nickel...

On May 22, Market Screener reported: "Brazil's Vale approves issuance of 6 billion reais in local debt."

Glencore [HK:805] [LSE:GLEN] (OTCPK:GLCNF)

On April 30, Glencore announced:

First Quarter Production Report 2025...Our full year 2025 production guidance remains unchanged...own sourced nickel production of 18,800 tonnes was in line with Q1 2024.

Glencore Q1 own-sourced production (source)

On May 9, Glencore announced: "2025 H1 Distribution... This repayment is of US$0.05 per ordinary share."

BHP Group [ASX:BHP] (NYSE:BHP)

BHP's Nickel West operations include the Mt Keith Nickel Mine in Australia. Nickel West produced 81.6 kt of nickel in the '24 financial year. In 2024, BHP temporarily suspended Western Australia Nickel operations due to low nickel prices. Stage 1 production of the Kwinana Nickel Refinery is aimed to be 100ktpa nickel sulphate.

On May 27, BHP announced: "BHP launches its first Industry AI Hub in Singapore to accelerate AI adoption in the mining and resources sector."

On May 27, Miningdotcom reported:

BHP refocuses on Finnmark Alliance with Kingsrose...The alliance will complete a prospectivity assessment to develop camps of intrusions for copper, nickel and platinum group element (PGE) sulphides as both companies believe the region holds potential for “significant” discoveries.

Anglo American [LSX:AAL] (OTCPK:AAUKF)

Anglo American's sale of their nickel business to MMG Singapore Resources continues to target completion by Q3 2025.

On April 30, Anglo American reported:

AGM 2025 – Address to Shareholders...The global macro-economic landscape continued to present challenges in 2024...The sale of the nickel business was agreed in February 2025 for up to $500 million in cash...

Eramet [FRA:ERA] (OTCPK:ERMAY)

On May 26, Eramet announced: "General Meeting of May 26, 2025: Appointment of Paulo Castellari as Chief Executive Officer..."

On May 28, Eramet announced:

Sherritt International (OTCPK:SHERF)[TSX:S]

On May 13, Sherritt International announced:
